title: Cardiovascular examination — identify the murmur
candidateInstructions: You are an FY2 doctor in cardiology outpatients. Mr Samuel Okafor, a 65-year-old man, has been referred by his GP with exertional breathlessness and a cardiac murmur detected on routine examination. Please perform a focused cardiovascular examination and present your findings to the examiner.
examinerNotes: Mitral regurgitation station. Candidate should correctly identify the pansystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla. Expected findings: displaced apex beat, soft S1, pansystolic murmur grade 3/6 at apex radiating to left axilla. Causes: mitral valve prolapse, ischemic MR.

patient.name: Samuel Okafor
patient.reasonForAttendance: My GP heard something wrong with my heart

hpi.character: Breathlessness climbing one flight of stairs

examination.general: Well-looking man, no distress at rest
examination.cardiovascular: JVP not elevated. Apex beat displaced to 6th intercostal space, anterior axillary line. Soft S1, normal S2. Pansystolic murmur grade 3/6 at apex radiating to left axilla. No thrills. No S3 on this occasion.

investigations.name: Echocardiogram
investigations.result: Left ventricular dilation. EF 45%. Moderate-severe mitral regurgitation with eccentric jet directed posteriorly. Mitral valve prolapse. No significant aortic valve disease.
investigations.interpretation: Moderate-severe mitral regurgitation due to mitral valve prolapse. Left ventricular dysfunction (EF 45%) — likely consequence of chronic volume overload plus prior MI.

investigations.name: 12-lead ECG
investigations.result: Sinus rhythm 76 bpm. P mitrale (bifid P waves in V1). Q waves V1-V3 (previous anterior MI). LVH by voltage criteria. No current ischaemia.
investigations.interpretation: LVH and P mitrale consistent with chronic mitral regurgitation and volume overload. Evidence of previous anterior MI.

patient.emotionalState: Calm and cooperative. Slightly anxious about what the doctor will find.
patient.communicationStyle: Cooperative, allows examination. Asks 'what did you find?' at the end.
doNotVolunteer: Details of previous MI unless asked

primaryDiagnosis: Mitral Regurgitation (moderate-severe) due to Mitral Valve Prolapse

dataGathering.criterion: Inspects correctly — jaundice, pallor, cyanosis, clubbing, splinter haemorrhages
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Systematic peripheral examination from hands to face

dataGathering.criterion: Palpates apex beat and identifies displacement
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Correctly identifies lateral displacement of apex beat

dataGathering.criterion: Auscultates correctly in all areas with diaphragm and bell
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Auscultates apex, lower left sternal edge, aortic area, pulmonary area
Correctly checks for axillary radiation of murmur

clinicalManagement.criterion: Correctly identifies murmur as pansystolic at apex
clinicalManagement.detailedExpectation: States: pansystolic, loudest at apex, radiates to axilla

interpersonalSkills.detailedExpectation: Introduces self, gains consent, offers chaperone
Positions patient at 45° for JVP assessment, then supine for examination
Clearly explains findings in lay terms

references.source: ESC Guidelines on valvular heart disease 2021

learningObjectives: Identify and characterise a pansystolic murmur at the apex
